sentences.base_language: My name is Audrey, I'm from Philadelphia or something
sentences.target_language: 私の名前はオードリーです。フィラデルフィア出身です、たぶん。
sentences.romanized: Watashi no namae wa Ōdorī desu. Firaderufia shusshin desu, tabun.
sentences.literal: My name Audrey is. Philadelphia from-born am, probably.
sentences.grammarNote:
**私の名前は〜です** (watashi no namae wa ~ desu) = 'my name is ~'. **の** is the possessive particle connecting **私** and **名前**.
**出身** (shusshin) = origin/hometown: **出** (shutsu, go out) + **身** (shin, self/body). Pattern: [place] + **出身です** = 'I am from [place]'.
**たぶん** (tabun) = 'probably / maybe' — a hedging word that softens a statement.

sentences.base_language: I have a lot of hobbies!
sentences.target_language: 趣味がたくさんあります！
sentences.grammarNote:
**趣味** (shumi) = hobby/interest: **趣** (shu, taste/inclination) + **味** (mi, flavor/interest).
**たくさん** (takusan) = a lot/many; placed before or after the noun it modifies.
**あります** (arimasu) = there is / I have (for inanimate things). **います** (imasu) is used for people and animals.

sentences.base_language: I like to ride bikes with my boyfriend Waller
sentences.target_language: 彼氏のウォーラーと自転車に乗るのが好きです。
sentences.romanized: Kareshi no Wōrā to jitensha ni noru no ga suki desu.
sentences.literal: Boyfriend Waller with bicycle ride like.
sentences.grammarNote:
**彼氏** (kareshi) = boyfriend: **彼** (kare, he/him) + **氏** (shi, honorific suffix). **彼女** (kanojo) = girlfriend or 'she'.
**の** (no) nominalizes the verb phrase **乗る** (to ride): **乗るの** = 'the act of riding'. This lets the whole clause take **が** as the subject of **好き**.
**〜のが好きです** = 'I like doing ~': the standard pattern for expressing hobby preferences.

sentences.base_language: I like to make crafts too
sentences.target_language: 手芸をするのも好きです。
sentences.romanized: Shugei wo suru no mo suki desu.
sentences.grammarNote:
**手芸** (shugei) = handicrafts/needlework: **手** (te, hand) + **芸** (gei, art/skill) — 'hand arts'.
**〜をするのも好き** = 'I also like doing ~'. The **も** replaces **が** to add 'also/too' to the subject: **のも好き** means this in addition to the already-mentioned hobby.
**〜をする** is how Japanese turns many nouns into verbs: **手芸をする** (do crafts), **料理をする** (cook), **勉強をする** (study).

sentences.base_language: What kind of crafts you might ask
sentences.target_language: どんな手芸かって聞くかもしれませんね。
sentences.romanized: Donna shugei katte kiku kamo shiremasen ne.
sentences.literal: What kind crafts-as-for ask might, right.
sentences.grammarNote: 〜かもしれない (kamo shirenai) = 'might / may / perhaps'. It expresses possibility without certainty. The ね at the end invites the listener into the narrative. 〜かって is a casual quotation particle: 'as for the question of what kind…'

sentences.base_language: Well the person writing this didn't ask so I'm guessing puppets
sentences.target_language: まあ、これを書いた人は聞かなかったので、たぶん人形劇かな。
sentences.romanized: Mā, kore wo kaita hito wa kikanakatta node, tabun ningyōgeki ka na.
sentences.literal: Well, this-wrote person didn't-ask so, probably puppets maybe.
sentences.grammarNote:
**書いた人** = 'the person who wrote [it]': a relative clause in Japanese comes before the noun it modifies, with no relative pronoun — just **[verb] + 人**.
**ので** (node) = because/since (giving an objective reason, slightly more formal than **から**).
**かな** at sentence end is a gentle wondering particle: 'I wonder if…' or 'maybe…?' — expressing soft speculation without strong assertion.

sentences.base_language: I also have a lot of plants
sentences.target_language: 植物もたくさん持っています。
sentences.grammarNote:
**植物** (shokubutsu) = plant(s): **植** (shoku, to plant) + **物** (butsu/mono, thing).
**持っています** (motte imasu) = have/possess (ongoing state): **持つ** (motsu, to hold/have) → te-form **持って** + **います**. Using **〜ています** for possession expresses a continuing state of ownership.
**も** replaces **は** or **が** to mean 'also/too': **植物も** = 'plants too (in addition to other things)'.

sentences.base_language: Like more plants than you'd ever imagine
sentences.target_language: 想像できないくらいたくさんの植物です。
sentences.romanized: Sōzō dekinai kurai takusan no shokubutsu desu.
sentences.grammarNote:
**想像** (sōzō) = imagination: **想** (sō, thought) + **像** (zō, image). **想像できない** = 'cannot imagine' (potential negative of **想像する**).
**〜くらい / 〜ほど** = 'to the degree that / so much that': **想像できないくらい** = 'to a degree you can't imagine'.
**の** between **たくさん** and **植物** connects them: **たくさんの植物** = 'a lot of plants' (many plants).

sentences.base_language: I work as a fortune teller
sentences.target_language: 占い師として働いています。
sentences.grammarNote:
**占い師** (uranaishi) = fortune teller: **占い** (uranai, divination/fortune-telling) + **師** (shi, expert/master). **師** appears in many professional titles: **医師** (isha, doctor), **教師** (kyōshi, teacher).
**として** (toshite) = 'as / in the role of': **〜として働く** = 'work as ~'. Use **として** for professional or functional identities.
**働いています** = is working (ongoing state): **働く** (hataraku) → te-form + **います**.

sentences.base_language: I can read tarot cards and predict the future
sentences.target_language: タロットカードを読んで未来を予言できます。
sentences.romanized: Tarotto kādo wo yonde mirai wo yogen dekimasu.
sentences.literal: Tarot cards read future predict can.
sentences.grammarNote:
**読んで** is the te-form of **読む** (yomu, to read), linking two sequential actions: 'read cards and (then) predict'.
**未来** (mirai) = future: **未** (mi, not yet) + **来** (rai, come) — 'what has not yet come'.
**予言** (yogen) = prediction/prophecy: **予** (yo, in advance) + **言** (gen, say). **できます** = can do (potential form of **する**).

sentences.base_language: I play dungeons and dragons with my best friends
sentences.target_language: 親友たちとダンジョンズ・アンド・ドラゴンズをやっています。
sentences.romanized: Shin'yū-tachi to Danjonzu Ando Doragonzu wo yatte imasu.
sentences.literal: Best-friends with Dungeons and Dragons playing.
sentences.grammarNote:
**親友** (shin'yū) = best friend/close friend: **親** (shin, close/parent) + **友** (yū, friend).
**たち** is a pluralizing suffix for people: **友達たち** (friends), **親友たち** (best friends).
**と** (to) = 'with (a companion)': **〜と一緒にやる** or simply **〜とやる** = 'do with ~'.
**やっています** = is playing/doing (ongoing): **やる** (to do) in te-form + **います**.

sentences.base_language: Other than that I am pretty chill
sentences.target_language: それ以外は、わりとのんびりしています。
sentences.romanized: Sore igai wa, warito nonbiri shite imasu.
sentences.grammarNote:
**それ以外** (sore igai) = 'other than that': **以外** (igai) = outside of/besides, used after a noun to mean 'other than ~'.
**わりと** (warito) = 'fairly/relatively' — a mild intensifier, softer than **とても** or **かなり**.
**のんびりしています** = is laid-back/relaxed: **のんびり** is an adverb for a leisurely, easygoing manner + **しています** (doing/being in that state).

sentences.target_language: 時々ベーグルを買います。
sentences.grammarNote:
**時々** (tokidoki) = sometimes: the kanji **時** (toki, time) doubled. Frequency adverbs in Japanese go near the start of the sentence or before the verb.
**ベーグル** (bēguru) = bagel — a katakana loanword. Katakana is used for foreign-origin words and gives a visual cue that the word is borrowed.
**買います** (kaimasu) = buy (polite present/habitual): **買う** (kau, to buy) → **買います**.

sentences.base_language: One day Jeff will ask more follow up questions to write more on this story
sentences.target_language: いつかジェフがもっと質問してくれて、この話が続くといいですね。
sentences.romanized: Itsuka Jefu ga motto shitsumon shite kurete, kono hanashi ga tsuzuku to ii desu ne.
sentences.literal: Someday Jeff more questions ask-give, this story continues would-be-good right.
sentences.grammarNote:
**してくれて** = 'does (for me/us) and…': **くれる** is a giving verb used when someone does something beneficial for the speaker or in-group. Te-form **くれて** links it to the next clause.
**〜といいですね** = 'it would be nice if ~': **と** (conditional) + **いい** (good) + **ですね** (seeking shared agreement). Expresses a hopeful wish.
**いつか** (itsuka) = 'someday/one day' — an indefinite future time.

sentences.base_language: But for now, that is about it
sentences.target_language: でも今のところ、こんな感じです。
sentences.romanized: Demo ima no tokoro, konna kanji desu.
sentences.grammarNote:
**今のところ** (ima no tokoro) = 'for now / at this point in time': **今** (ima, now) + **の** (possessive) + **ところ** (tokoro, place/point) — 'the point that is now'.
**こんな** = 'this kind of / like this': a demonstrative adjective modifying nouns (compare **この** before a specific noun).
**感じ** (kanji) = feeling/sense/vibe (noun): **こんな感じです** = 'it's this kind of feeling / that's about the gist of it' — a very natural wrap-up phrase.
